coredata.dc:title: Novel coal-based carbon encapsulating Co-N-C derived from ZIF-67 composite as an efficient chainmail electrocatalyst for zinc-air batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Hongtao
Lei, Jing
Li, Qin
Chen, Si
Nassr, Abu Bakr
Geng, Qin
Li, Yizhao
coredata.dc:description: Energy shortages and the global climate crisis have led researchers to develop renewable clean energy sources. Zinc-air batteries have attracted much attention due to their high theoretical energy density, safety, and conversion efficiency. However, the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) exhibits slow reaction kinetics and high reaction energy barriers, which require catalysts with high activity, selectivity, and stability to improve the reaction rate. Chainmail catalysts have attracted attention in the field of energy conversion and storage due to their structural stability. In this paper, coal-based carbon encapsulating Co-N-C chainmail electrocatalysts are designed and constructed, which can effectively reduce the thermal crumpling phenomenon of the framework structure, enhance the defect content and provide active sites for ORR. The results show that G/Co-N-C-0.025 exhibits a higher half-wave potential than that of Pt/C, with a high ultimate current density of 4.6 mA cm−2, good cycling stability, and high methanol resistance. A primary zinc-air battery is assembled, with an open-circuit voltage of 1.417 V and a maximum power density of 82 mW cm−2. The specific capacity is 789.2 mAh g−1 at a current of 20 mA cm−2. This work provides new ideas for the design and application of high-performance chainmail electrocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Coal-based carbon encapsulating Co-N-C
Chainmail electrocatalyst
Oxygen reduction reaction
Zinc-air battery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of the ratio of ZIF-67 and GQDs
ORR catalytic activity study based on the ratio of ZIF-67 and GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra and SEM images of (c) G/Co-N-C-0.0125, (d) G/Co-N-C-0.025, (e) G/Co-N-C-0.05, and (f) G/Co-N-C-0.2.

(A) LSV curves and (b) the histogram of E 1/2 and j of G/Co-N-C-0.2, G/Co-N-C-0.05,G/Co-N-C-0.025 and G/Co-N-C-0.0125 in O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 with a rotating speed of 1600 rpm; (c) The calculated Cdl values vs. scan rates; (d) LSV curves of Co-N-C, G/Co-N-C-0.025 and Pt/C as the control group.

(A) CV curves of Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025 in N2- and O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1; (b) The histogram of E 1/2 and j, (c) Tafel curves of Co-N-C, G/Co-N-C-0.025 and Pt/C with a rotating speed of 1600 rpm LSV curves; (d) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025.
Low-magnification and high-magnification TEM images of (a, b) Co-N-C and (c, d) G/Co-N-C-0.025.
(A) XPS spectra of Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025; (b) The contents of N and O elemental corresponding to XPS; (c) N 1s and (d) Co 2p spectra.

(A) LSV curves of G/Co-N-C-0.025 and Pt/C in 0.1 mol L−1 KOH at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 with rotating speed of 1600 rpm by RRDE; (b) Electron transfer number n and H2O2 yield; (c) I-t curves obtained by chronoamperometry in O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH; (d) Methanol tolerance curves with addition of 1 mol L−1 methanol.

(A) Open-circuit potential test of G/Co-N-C-0.025 and Pt/C catalysts, the inset is open-circuit potential measured by multimeter; (b) Polarization and power density curves, (c) Specific capacity curves of G/Co-N-C-0.025 and Pt/C catalysts, (d) discharge curves at different current densities of G/Co-N-C-0.025.
Schematic illustration for the preparation of the G/Co-N-C.

head.ce:title: Novel coal-based carbon encapsulating Co-N-C derived from ZIF-67 composite as an efficient chainmail electrocatalyst for zinc-air batteries

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huzhou Key Laboratory of Smart and Clean Energy
University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Energy shortages and the global climate crisis have led researchers to develop renewable clean energy sources. Zinc-air batteries have attracted much attention due to their high theoretical energy density, safety, and conversion efficiency. However, the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) exhibits slow reaction kinetics and high reaction energy barriers, which require catalysts with high activity, selectivity, and stability to improve the reaction rate. Chainmail catalysts have attracted attention in the field of energy conversion and storage due to their structural stability. In this paper, coal-based carbon encapsulating Co-N-C chainmail electrocatalysts are designed and constructed, which can effectively reduce the thermal crumpling phenomenon of the framework structure, enhance the defect content and provide active sites for ORR. The results show that G/Co-N-C-0.025 exhibits a higher half-wave potential than that of Pt/C, with a high ultimate current density of 4.6 mA cm−2, good cycling stability, and high methanol resistance. A primary zinc-air battery is assembled, with an open-circuit voltage of 1.417 V and a maximum power density of 82 mW cm−2. The specific capacity is 789.2 mAh g−1 at a current of 20 mA cm−2. This work provides new ideas for the design and application of high-performance chainmail electrocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive exploitation and extensive consumption of fossil fuels have brought about energy shortages and global climate crises that seriously threaten the ecological environment and human health [1,2]. To realize the sustainable development of human existence, the search for renewable and clean energy has attracted extensive attention from researchers [3,4]. Renewable energy sources such as wind and solar energy have been widely investigated in the past decades, but geographical limitations and fluctuations have restricted their large-scale commercialization [5]. How to convert and store renewable clean energy has become one of the main ways to meet future energy demand.

Zinc-air batteries have become a promising candidate for energy conversion devices, relying on the redox reaction between the anode (metallic zinc) and the cathode (oxygen) to convert chemical energy into electrical energy [6–8]. It has attracted much attention due to its simple cell structure, good safety, high energy density, high conversion efficiency, and non-pollution [9,10]. Due to the open structure of zinc-air batteries, cathode O2 is supplied from the outside and not stored inside the battery, thus having a higher energy density than commercial lead-acid batteries, lithium-ion batteries, etc., with a theoretical energy density of 1350 W h kg−1 [11,12]. However, the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is a four-electron reaction involving multiple electron gains and losses with a slow kinetic rate, leading to result in a high overpotential at the cathodic ORR, limiting the maximum power density of the battery [13]. Meanwhile, the two-electron and four-electron reactions of the ORR occur simultaneously, and the product of the two-electron process, H2O2, is corrosive, which not only reduces the energy storage efficiency of the battery but also corrodes the electrode materials [14,15]. Thus, The development of oxygen reduction electrocatalysts with high selectivity and high stability becomes particularly important.

Precious metal Pt catalysts are considered to be the most efficient catalysts capable of realizing 4e-ORR, and Pt/C catalysts are currently the only commercial catalysts for ORR, but their high price and poor stability have hindered their large-scale application in the commercial field [16]. In recent years, transition metal catalysts have shown good activity for ORR and can greatly reduce the cost of catalysts [17,18]. Several studies have proposed that transition metal-nitrogen-carbon (M-N-C) can be used as an active catalyst for ORR by providing more active sites for the reaction [19–22]. M-N-C is usually obtained by pyrolysis of a carbon substrate, a nitrogen source, and a transition metal source at high temperatures, which ensures the graphitization of the carbon material but the metal is susceptible to deactivation by agglomeration [23–25]. Typically, a high level of nitrogen doping, high conductivity, and well-distributed active sites are crucial for enhancing the catalytic activity of M-N-C for ORR [26,27]. Meanwhile, the inherent defects of carbon-based electrocatalysts contribute significantly to the enhancement of electrocatalytic activity [28]. High-temperature pyrolysis is the key to generating M-N-C materials with high electrical conductivity and large specific surface area [29]. However, the N content of the resulting catalysts usually decreases with increasing carbonization temperature [30]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a class of highly ordered porous materials composed of metal ions/clusters and organic ligands, which have the advantages of well-defined metal nodes, modifiable surface functional groups, etc., and are promising for ORR catalysis [31–33]. However, the zeolite imidazolium frameworks are subject to morphological crumpling or even collapse upon high-temperature carbonization, which may lead to a decrease in the catalytic performance [34].

In recent years, chainmail catalysts have attracted wide interest in energy conversion and storage applications due to their structural stability [35–37]. Due to the extra layer of protection, the catalytical active sites are well protected from corrosion or phase transition due to the external reaction environment [38]. At the same time, this synergistic effect of maintaining the core-shell structure also enhances the catalytic activity of certain reactions [39–41]. The chainmail material used for catalysts should not only have the ability to form ionic or covalent bonds with reactant molecules to facilitate the electron transfer from the core catalyst to the surface interface [35], but also be a robust and flexible material that can fully encapsulate the catalyst nanoparticles and form a physical isolation from the external reaction environment [42].

Carbon-based materials with good chemical stability and special electronic properties are promising candidates as chainmail materials [43]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based materials with dimensions less than 10 nm [44]. Not only do they retain the chemical inertness of graphene and have more stable physicochemical properties, but they are also rich in edge sites, which are more prone to electrocatalytic reactions than sites on a flat surface [45]. Coal-based GQDs are easier to prepare in large quantities than ordinary GQDs. Tour et al. proposed a new chemical oxidation method for the large-scale preparation of GQDs from cheap and abundant coal [46]. Coal-based GQDs are not only rich in N species, but also porous nitrogen-doped carbon nanosheets with abundant defects can be obtained by a simple treatment [47]. This provides a new idea for the design of chainmail catalysts.

In this work, ZIF-67 was utilized to adsorb coal-based GQDs, and coal-based carbon encapsulating Co-N-C chainmail electrocatalysts were obtained by annealing treatment, which can effectively reduce the thermal crumpling phenomenon of the MOF structure, and at the same time enhance the defect content and provide active sites for the ORR. The results show that G/Co-N-C-0.025 exhibits a higher half-wave potential (E 1/2 = 0.87 V), and a high ultimate current density compared to that of Pt/C. A primary zinc-air battery was assembled, with an open-circuit voltage of 1.406 V and a maximum power density of 82 mW cm−2. The specific capacity was 640 mAh g−1 at a current of 20 mA cm−2. This work provides new ideas for the design and application of high-performance chainmail electrocatalysts.

Preparation of GQDs: Coal-based GQDs were prepared by a modified chemical oxidation method as described in the literature [47]. Briefly, 20 mL of mixed acid (VHNO3:VH2SO4 = 3:1, HNO3 and H2SO4 are 63 % and 98 % acids, respectively) was slowly added to 1 g of fine bituminous coals and refluxed at 80 °C for 10 h. Then it was separated by centrifugation and washed with a large of water. Finally, the GQDs were purified and obtained by dialysis.

Preparation of ZIF-67: 0.8 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of methanol. 1.9 g of dimethylimidazole was also dissolved in 20 mL of methanol. Cobalt nitrate solution was quickly injected into dimethylimidazole solution under stirring. After stirring for 0.5 h, the mixture was then left to stand for 8 h. It was filtered and washed with methanol three times and then dried at 50°C. The purple color ZIF-67 was obtained.

Preparation of Co-N-C: The precursor (ZIF-67) was put into the corundum boat in the tube furnace under the Ar atmosphere, heated at a heating rate of 2°C min−1 to 800°C, holding for 2 h at this temperature to obtain the Co-N-C material.

Preparation of G/Co-N-C-0.025: The prepared GQDs were dispersed into methanol to form a solution with a concentration of 0.05 mg mL−1100 mg of the prepared ZIF-67 was dispersed uniformly into 20 mL of distilled water and ultrasonically dispersed evenly. 5 mL of GQDs solution was added dropwise under stirring conditions, and the mixture was stirred for 1 h. The GQDs/ZIF67–0.025 precursor was collected by centrifugation and washed with water. The GQDs/ZIF67–0.025 precursor was put into a corundum boat in a tube furnace under the Ar atmosphere, and the temperature was increased to 800°C at a rate of 2°C min−1 and the temperature was kept at 800°C for 2 h A black G/Co-N-C-0.025 was obtained.

The synthesis procedure was consistent with the above steps except for the added amount of GQDs. The added amounts of GQDs were 40 mL, 10 mL, and 3 mL for synthesizing G/Co-N-C-0.2, G/Co-N-C-0.05, and G/Co-N-C-0.0125, respectively. Moreover, the Co-N-C catalyst was prepared under identical conditions without additional GQDs for the sake of comparison.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8) is employed to examine the crystal structure of prepared samples at a scanning region of 10°-80°. Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected with HORIBA NANO and ESCALAB 250 Xi spectrometer, respectively. To analyze the microscopic morphology and structure of the prepared samples, field-emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800H), transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM-2010F) were employed.
The detail of electrochemical measurements was presented in the Supporting Information.

The coal-based GQDs were prepared via a modified chemical oxidation method, and TEM images are shown in Fig. S1 [47]. The GQDs with uniform size can be observed, with a diameter of about 5 nm. The preparation process of the catalyst is displayed in Scheme 1 where the ZIF-67 was used as the precursor of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. S2a, the XRD pattern indicates the pure phase properties of the precursor. SEM images of the precursor show a rhombic dodecahedron crystal with about 500 nm. To further optimize the catalyst, the initial investigation focuses on the influence of the catalyst structure based on the ratio of ZIF-67 to GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the ratio of ZIF-67 and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Co-N-C, obtained by carbonization of pure ZIF-67 at 800°C, maintains a rhombic dodecahedral structure, but the surface is severely crumpled, as shown in Fig. S3. The ZIF-67 precursor was used to adsorb different contents of GQD on its surface, and after the adsorption of GQD, the color of the adsorbed sample turned purple-black. As displayed in Fig. S4a and b, alayer of GQDs can be seen wrapped around the surface of the rhombic dodecahedron ZIF-67, resulting in a surface that is not as smooth as pure ZIF-67. The black powders were obtained after carbonization at 800°C, which were labeled G/Co-N-C-0.2, G/Co-N-C-0.05, G/Co-N-C-0.025, and G/Co-N-C-0.0125, respectively. The phase compositions of samples were characterized by XRD analysis in Fig. 1 a and Fig. S5a. All samples show peaks at 44°, 52°, and 76°, which are characterized by (111), (200), and (220) planes of metallic cobalt [48]. The variations in peak intensities indicate that there are some differences in the degree of crystallization of the materials [49]. Raman spectra show that the D peak at 1350 cm−1 and G peak at 1600 cm−1 can be seen in all samples (Fig. 1b and Fig. S5b). The D peak is related to defects in the carbon interlayers and the G peak is related to the crystallinity of the sp2 carbon, so the I D/I G value is usually used to indicate the degree of defects of the sample [50]. The I D/I G values of G/Co-N-C-0.2, G/Co-N-C-0.05, G/Co-N-C-0.025, and G/Co-N-C-0.0125 are 1.02, 1.01, 1.01, and 0.89, respectively, and the higher I D/I G values indicate higher relative defect density.

The SEM images of G/Co-N-C-x were shown in Fig. 1(c–f) to characterize the microscopic morphology and structure. The SEM image of G/Co-N-C-0.0125 showed the surface of the dodecahedron is relatively smooth without rough small particles (Fig. 1c). When the amount of GQDs is increased, the morphology of G/Co-N-C-0.025 is shown in Fig. 1d, the G/Co-N-C-0.025 still maintains the dodecahedral structure with some rough particles but no breakage or crumbling. When the amount of GQDs is further increased, the surface of G/Co-N-C-0.05 was again relatively smooth (Fig. 1e), but some particles were still visible, and a small number of dodecahedrons were disrupted. From Fig. 1f, it can be observed that more nanoparticles are attached to the surface of G/Co-N-C-0.2 with further increasing the content of GQDs, and a large number of dodecahedral structures are disrupted. This may be due to the interaction forces between Co in the ZIF-67 skeleton and N in the GQDs when excess GQDs are encapsulated on the ZIF-67 surface. It suggests that GQDs modification of ZIF-67 will inhibit the crumpling of ZIF-67 to a certain extent because of the small size effect and surface functional groups of GQDs [51]. However, too many GQDs can lead to the combination of N in GQDs and Co in ZIF-67, which could dissociate the dodecahedral structure. N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves are displayed in Fig. S6 and the corresponding data are listed in Table S1. The specific surface area of the individual Co-N-C is 280.13 m2 g−1. The encapsulation by carbon layers leads to a certain degree of reduction in the material's specific surface area, but it does not significantly affect the pore size distribution, which remains predominantly in the range of 3–5 nm for mesopores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ORR catalytic activity study based on the ratio of ZIF-67 and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrocatalytic performance of different catalysts G/Co-N-C-0.2, G/Co-N-C-0.05, G/Co-N-C-0.025, and G/Co-N-C-0.0125 was tested and evaluated. The LSV measurements were carried out in O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH at a sweep rate of 10 mV s−1, and the LSV polarization curves are shown in Fig. 2 a. It's clear from the figure that the G/Co-N-C-0.025 exhibits the most positive half-wave potential and higher limiting current indicating the highest catalytic activity among all catalysts. The corresponding histograms of half-wave potentials and limiting current densities are shown in Fig. 2b. The half-wave potential (E 1/2 = 0.87 V) and limiting current density (j = 4.6 mA cm−2) of G/Co-N-C-0.025 are significantly higher than those of the other catalysts with different GQDs ratios as the following: (G/Co-N-C-0.2 (E 1/2 = 0.83 V, j = 3.9 mA cm−2) < G/Co-N-C-0.05 (E 1/2 = 0.84 V, j = 3.9 mA cm−2) < G/Co-N-C-0.025 (E 1/2 = 0.85 V, j = 4.1 mA cm−2)). To identify the reason behind the higher catalytic activity, the electrochemical surface area expressed in the double layer capacitance (Cdl) was measured by cyclic voltammetry, The CV curves of G/Co-N-C-0.2, G/Co-N-C-0.05, G/Co-N-C-0.025, and G/Co-N-C-0.0125 at different scanning speeds are shown in Fig. S7, and the corresponding slope plots are shown in Fig. 2c. It's obvious that the G/Co-N-C-0.025 has the largest electrochemical active area, indicating that it has the highest catalytic activity. By comparing the LSV curves of Pt/C and Co-N-C, as shown in Fig. 2d, with that of G/Co-N-C-0.025, it's evident that G/Co-N-C-0.025 exhibits better ORR activity than Pt/C and uncoated Co-N-C, with a half-wave potential of 0.87 V. Additionally, the XRD patterns of Co-N-C also shows peaks at 44°, 52° and 76°, which characterized by (111), (200) and (220) planes of metallic cobalt, the same characteristics peaks displayed by G/Co-N-C-0.025. However, Raman spectra of Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025 indicate the I D/I G value of G/Co-N-C-0.025 is higher than Co-N-C, suggesting a higher defect density in G/Co-N-C-0.025 resulted from the addition of GQDs. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S8, G/Co-N-C-0.025 has a larger electrochemical active area than Co-N-C, indicating a higher electrocatalytic activity, the results that reflect the beneficial role of the addition of GQDs by providing more accessible active sites for ORR and therefore the higher catalytic activity.

The CV curves of the samples were tested in N2- and O2-saturated 0.1 mol L−1 KOH solutions, respectively, at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 to further determine the ORR activity of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the curves of Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025 under N2 saturation did not show reduction peaks, and a clear oxygen reduction peak appeared in the O2-saturated electrolyte solution indicating significant catalytic activity of G/Co-N-C-0.025 on ORR [25]. The reduction peak for G/Co-N-C-0.025 was more defined and larger with a more positive potential which also indicates that G/Co-N-C-0.025 has a higher oxygen reduction activity than the Co-N-C. Fig. 3b shows the corresponding histograms of half-wave potentials and limiting current densities for the control experiments, from which it can be seen that the half-wave potentials (E 1/2 = 0.87 V) and limiting current densities (j = 4.6 mA cm−2) of G/Co-N-C-0.025 are higher than that of the Co-N-C material (E 1/2 = 0.84 V, j = 4.2 mA cm−2), and can also be comparable to Pt/C commercial catalysts. To evaluate the kinetic rates of the catalysts, the Tafel slopes were calculated for Co-N-C, G/Co-N-C-0.025, and Pt/C (Fig. 3c), corresponding to 105 mV dec−1, 99 mV dec−1 and 99 mV dec−1, respectively. This indicates that G/Co-N-C-0.025 has faster reaction kinetics than Co-N-C, and the same kinetic rate as Pt/C [52]. The electrochemical impedance plots of Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025 were carried out at open circuit potential in the frequency range of 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz, respectively (Fig. 3d), which was fitted to a suitable equivalent circuit model (the inset of Fig. 3d). The overall trend of the EIS results is similar indicating that both of these types of N-C materials have excellent electrochemical performance, while in the high-frequency region, the semicircle radius of G/Co-N-C-0.025 is smaller indicating that the chainmail electrocatalyst with carbon layer has lower interfacial transfer resistance which can improve the interfacial reaction kinetics, and thus enhance the ORR activity [53].

The microscopic morphology of the chainmail electrocatalyst was further characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4a, the morphology of alone Co-N-C derived from ZIF-67 cannot be complete rhombic dodecahedron, while black Co nanoparticles with large size are present inside. Porous carbon structures can also be seen in the edge region (Fig. 4b). In contrast, when CQDs are introduced, the G/Co-N-C-0.025 obtained by annealing exhibits a complete rhombic dodecahedron structure, along with black Co nanoparticles of smaller sizes inside (Fig. 4c). A double-layer carbon layer structure can be seen in the edge region, indicating that such a chainmail core-shell structure can enhance electron transfer and inhibit the agglomeration of metal nanoparticles [38].

The chemical composition of the sample surface was analyzed by XPS. In the survey spectrum of XPS (Fig. 5 a), the surface elements of Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025 of C, N, O, and Co elements were detected. The corresponding elemental content histograms are shown in Fig. 5b, from which it can be seen that the introduction of trace amounts of GQDs has little effect on the elemental content distribution on the surface of ZIF-67 derivatives. The N 1s spectra of Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025 are shown in Fig. 5c, indicating that the element N exists in four structural forms, including pyridine N (398.1 eV), Co-N (399.7 eV), pyrrole N (399.9 eV), and graphite N (400.9 eV), respectively. Among them, the pyridine N, graphite N, and Co-N structures are all considered to be active sites for ORR [54]. The Co 2p spectra of Co-N-C and G/Co-N-C-0.025 resulted in Fig. 5d, where the binding energies located at 778.6 eV and 794.10 eV belong to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of metal Co. The peaks at 796.1 eV and 780.0 eV correspond to the 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 of the Co-O bond, which is attributed to the surface oxidation of the material [55]. The peaks at 786.7 eV and 802.4 eV are attributed to the satellite peaks, and the binding energy at 796.1 eV is attributed to the Co-N bond. By comparing the contents, it is found that the Co-N content of G/Co-N-C-0.025 is higher than that of Co-N-C, indicating that the introduction of GQDs can form more Co-N bonds in the material.

To further characterize the electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst and its selectivity for 4e-ORR, the LSV curves of G/Co-N-C-0.025 were tested in 0.1 mol L−1 KOH solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 using RRDE electrode and compared to that of Pt/C as shown in Fig. 6 a. The H2O2 selectivity and electron transfer number (n) of the catalysts were calculated from the RRDE test results according to Formulae 1 and 2 in the Supporting Information. The results of G/Co-N-C-0.025 in Fig. 6b showed that the electron transfer number of G/Co-N-C-0.025 was 3.85 in the potential range of 0.2–0.8 V, which is similar to the electron transfer number of Pt/C, indicating that G/Co-N-C-0.025 mainly undergoes four-electron reaction. In the potential range of 0.2–0.8 V, the average H2O2 selectivity is 7.3 %, and the highest H2O2 selectivity is not more than 10 %, which further proves that the four-electron reaction process occurs on G/Co-N-C-0.025 catalyst, the behavior that characteristic for noble metal-based catalysts.

The stability test of G/Co-N-C-0.025 was carried out by chronoamperometry at 0.5 V for 5 h to evaluate the long-term stability of the catalyst. The i-t plot (Fig. 6c) shows that G/Co-N-C-0.025 has good stability, and the current retention rate reaches 91.4 % after 5 h, which is better than that of Pt/C (current retention rate of 83.7 %), indicating that G/Co-N-C-0.025 has good stability. Adding 1 mol L−1 methanol into the electrolyte, the stability test of G/Co-N-C-0.025 against methanol was carried out (Fig. 6d), and its tolerance to methanol was significantly better than that of Pt/C, and the current retention rate of G/Co-N-C-0.025 was 93.9 % after 1 h.

To test the applicability of the catalyst as a cathode electrode for the primary Zinc-air battery, the zinc-air battery was assembled using carbon paper coated with G/Co-N-C-0.025 catalyst and commercial Pt/C catalyst at a loading of 1 mg cm−2 as the cathode, 0.5 mm zinc plate as the anode, and 6 mol L−1 KOH as the electrolyte, and was tested and evaluated for practical applications. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the open-circuit voltage of the assembled zinc-air battery with G/Co-N-C-0.025 catalyst and commercial Pt/C catalyst was measured to be 1.417 V and 1.420 V using a multimeter, and the voltage was still maintained at 1.417 V after 900 s of cycling (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig. 7b, the specific capacity of G/Co-N-C-0.025 catalyst and Pt/C catalyst was calculated to be 789.2 mAh g−1, 781.9 mAh g−1at a current of 20 mA cm−2 by normalizing the zinc plate consumption, respectively. From the polarization and power density curves (Fig. 7c), the power density of G/Co-N-C-0.025 was 82 mW cm−2, superior to that of Pt/C catalysts (78 mW cm−2). As the discharge current density increases from 2 to 10 mA cm−2, the discharge voltage plateau gradually decreases. When the current density is reduced back to 2 mA cm−2, the discharge voltage plateau rises back to its original value, demonstrating excellent rate performance and reversibility.

In this work, we have functionalized ZIF-67 with coal-GQDs, preparing coal-based carbon encapsulated Co-N-C chainmail electrocatalysts through an annealing process at 800 °C. The protective effect of the carbon layer formed by self-polymerization of the GQDs effectively mitigates the structural collapse of ZIF-67 during high-temperature pyrolysis. Moreover, the edge-rich and defect-enriched nature of the GQDs not only augments the defect density of the resulting G/Co-N-C material but also enriches the active sites available for the ORR. The results of the structural and electrochemical characterization have led to the following conclusions: G/Co-N-C-0.025 exhibits a higher half-wave potential compared to that of Pt/C with a value of E 1/2 = 0.87 V, It also exhibits a high ultimate current density of 4.6 mA cm−2, and a significantly better stability and high methanol-resistance surpassing that of Pt/C. A primary zinc-air battery was assembled having an open-circuit voltage of 1.417 V and a maximum power density of 82 mW cm−2. The specific capacity was 789.2 mAh g−1 at 20 mA cm−2. This work provides an essential research avenue for the development of efficient and stabilized non-precious metal-based ORR electrocatalysts, and the design of high-performance Zn-air batteries.

Hongtao Xie: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Jing Lei: Formal analysis, Data curation. Qin Li: Formal analysis, Data curation. Si Chen: Writing – review & editing. Abu Bakr Nassr: Writing – review & editing. Qin Geng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yizhao Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Key R&D Program of Zhejiang (No. 2024C03136), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52100166, 22202032, and 22306023) and the Research Initiation Project of Yangtze Delta Region Institute (Huzhou) of UESTC (No. U03210037).
